resources.string: Do not treat any single result as a final legal, compliance, warranty, or security conclusion.
Duck Detector provides reference signals only. %1$s Always combine the app\'s output with manual verification, upstream evidence, and your own risk assessment before taking action.

By using Duck Detector, you agree to the following terms:
1. Purpose of use This application is designed solely for educational and personal security-assessment purposes. It helps users understand the security status of their own devices.
2. No warranty This application is provided "as is" without any warranties, express or implied. The developers do not guarantee the accuracy, completeness, or reliability of detection results.
3. User responsibility You are solely responsible for how you use this application and any consequences that may arise from its use.
4. Compliance with laws You agree to use this application in compliance with all applicable local, national, and international laws and regulations.

IMPORTANT NOTICE:
1. Detection limitations Duck Detector uses various methods to detect device security status, but no detection method is 100% accurate. Results should be considered indicators rather than definitive assessments.
2. False positives and false negatives The application may produce false positives or false negatives. Always use professional security tools and your own verification for comprehensive assessment.
3. No liability The developers, contributors, and distributors of Duck Detector shall not be held liable for any direct, indirect, incidental, special, or consequential damages arising from the use or inability to use this application.
4. Third-party services TEE revocation checks use a built-in snapshot by default. If you allow online revocation refresh, the app may contact the Google attestation revocation endpoint. Security scans themselves still run locally on your device, and detector findings are not intentionally uploaded as scan telemetry.

Privacy summary: Security checks run locally on your device. Detector findings are not intentionally uploaded as scan telemetry. TEE revocation checks use a built-in snapshot by default; if you allow online revocation refresh, the app may contact the Google attestation revocation endpoint.
By continuing, you acknowledge that you have read and agree to these terms.
Runtime controls for online verification paths.
TEE revocation checks always use the built-in snapshot; this only permits fetching Google\'s latest attestation revocation feed.
Changes apply immediately to online refresh behavior. Built-in TEE revocation checks remain enabled.
Checks run locally on-device. Built-in TEE revocation data is bundled with the app; online CRL refresh is optional and only happens if you enable it in Settings.
Copy version, build time and build hash
Contributed UI, C++, ASM and Kotlin.
Swipe left or right to browse authors.
Swipe left or right to browse authors. %1$d/%2$d
Browse bundled third-party projects and inspect their license notices.
Third-party components bundled in this build.
Tap any dependency to inspect license text and upstream project links.
This build is an alpha testing build. Because it includes a large-scale refactor touching C++ and assembly, unstable crashes may occur.
Unexpected startup failures or runtime crashes can still happen in this branch.
If you hit a problem, please report it. Clear reproduction steps are especially useful.
If you do not know how to capture crash logs, download AppErrorsTracking from one of the links below.
Crash Log Helper: AppErrorsTracking is available on GitHub Actions and Telegram CI.
You can dismiss this warning now.
If the screenshot does not contain detailed information, this issue will not be handled.
Provide complete information to get help.
Danger and warning cards were expanded automatically for full review.
Preferences and PackageManager visibility are still being loaded. Detector ViewModels stay paused until this completes.

Finish the required choices before detector scans begin. Notifications, Live Update routing, CRL online refresh, and PackageManager visibility are reviewed here in one place. The detector pipeline remains blocked until every required card below is settled.
Reading consent stores and PackageManager visibility before any detector starts.
Duck Detector can publish scan-progress notifications while detector cards collect evidence.
Choose whether scan progress may post notifications.
Android %1$d requires runtime notification permission. Startup scanning stays blocked until you allow it or explicitly continue without it.
This Android version does not require a runtime notification prompt, but the startup policy still needs to record your choice.
Scan progress will stay inside the app until you grant notification permission later.
Promoted ongoing notifications are not available on this Android version.
Duck Detector will use a regular notification path when notification permission is available.
Live Update depends on the notification path.
Grant notification permission first. If you keep notifications disabled, this card will fall back to regular in-app progress only.
On supported Android versions, scan progress can surface through the richer Live Update path.
Choose whether to use the Android Live Update path.
Duck Detector can open the system page for promoted ongoing notifications. If you skip this, scan progress falls back to a regular notification.
Promoted ongoing notifications stay disabled. Scan progress will use the regular notification path instead.
Choose whether TEE revocation checks may fetch online updates.
Duck Detector always checks the built-in revocation snapshot. Allowing network lets it fetch Google\'s latest attestation revocation feed during TEE validation.
TEE validation checks the built-in snapshot and may refresh Google\'s attestation revocation feed whenever network conditions permit.
TEE revocation checks use the built-in snapshot.
Duck Detector will not perform online CRL refresh until you re-enable it in Settings. Built-in revocation checks remain active.
Package inventory looks scoped or filtered.
PackageManager exposed only %1$d installed packages. Dangerous Apps and related probes can under-report installed tools under restricted visibility, scoped inventory, or HMA-style filtering.
Current visible package count: %1$d. Dangerous Apps findings may be conservative under this inventory scope.
PackageManager looks full but the visible inventory is unusually small.
Current visible package count: %1$d. Startup will continue, but Dangerous Apps will review this low-inventory condition as potential filtering evidence.
Current visible package count: %1$d. Dangerous Apps can use the standard package inventory path.
